1. No poverty.summary: No supporting evidence was found in the provided context to determine the proportion of the population living below the international poverty line. The context mentions various statistics, such as the average Timorese family utilizing 24 kg of forest products for cooking per day (Document: GS4210_Final-Monitoring-Report+07+May+2024.pdf, Page: 7) and the amount of farmer payments and community project funds paid into the Baguia community economy (Document: CCIPL+1481%28A%29+_Final+Performance+Certification+Report+-CC.pdf, Page: 26), but none of these statistics relate to poverty levels. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

Baguia.

data and parameters monitored include the number of trees planted of each species in each

Zero hunger.summary: No supporting evidence was found to determine the percentage change in Soil Organic Carbon (SOC) stock in agricultural lands within the project area during the reporting period. The context mentions that the project used the GS A/R Soil Carbon Guideline and did not change the method for estimating soil carbon since the last verification, but it does not provide the necessary data to calculate the percentage change in SOC stock. Additionally, no supporting evidence was found to determine the baseline SOC stock level in agricultural lands within the project area before the implementation of sustainable soil management practices, or the SOC stock level in agricultural lands within the project area after the implementation of sustainable soil management practices. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

The scope of this 
verification includes assessing the project activities, SDGs monitoring and 
CO2 Fixation over the period 1 March 2017 to 28 February 2019.
Since 
the start of the Project in 2010, 885 farmers have been involved in this 
Project by planting and managing over 155,000 seedlings on their 
farmland between 2010 to 2018.
The objective of this verification is to assess the Project’s actual carbon

Risk Area 1: Audit Findings | The Project has changed the Project Area in the reporting period  between 1 March 2017 to 28 Feb 2019.: The review of the Project documents, maps and interview with  the Project Developer, WithOneSeed (WOS) staff and the  participating farmers confirmed that the Project Area has not  experienced any damage due to natural disturbance such as fire  or wind in the reporting period.  The Auditor did not observe any damage or loss of tree  plantation in the Project Area during the site visit to the farmer’s  land. In addition, the GIS files (Land_Use_Cropland.shp;  Land_Use_Forest.shp; Land_Use_Grasslands.shp) showed no  change in the Project Area.  The review of the documentation, GIS files and the interview  with the PD, the participating farmers, and WOS staff concur  that the project area has not changed since the last verification.

CAR #:: In order to accurately calculate soil and baseline carbon stocks, the project area was  firstly stratified into two modeling units (MUs) according to land use type, i.e.  grassland and croplands. The GS recommended a soil dataset to assess the need to  stratify based on soil type, which was employed (refer to 401.13-AR-T-Baseline  March 2019.pdf). The assessment showed no variation in major soil type across the  project area and as such, further stratification based on soil type was not conducted.    Currently, CO2 Fixation estimates are based on one linear growth rate per species,  derived from the average diameter estimate for each tree age for trees planted  across the two MUs and a range of elevations between 2010 to 2018. The  measurements account for 14% of the total trees planted to date (refer to GS4210  Growth Model to 2018 v1.0.xlsx).  This has been conducted for Mahogany only at  present.  For each monitoring event, the standard deviation was calculated and was  observed to be up to 4 cm variation for a given tree age (note as the auditor  observed, likely to be due to elevation).  The standard error derived (using 90%  confidence interval) did not exceed 3%, except for measurements for the 2010 trees  where the sample size was low. In addition, the relationship of average diameter  with tree age (which accommodates the variation) showed an R2 value of 0.99,  which is exceptionally high. The average growth rates were also compared between  trees measured in croplands and those measured in grasslands and there was  minimal observed variation in the growth rates between grassland and croplands  (refer to 6.1 - Template - Carbon Performance_April 2019.pdf). Therefore, it was | CAR 02/19:

: Status of  CAR/FAR/OBS | for estimating the carbon stock.   The Project has tree plantations with age cohorts from 2010 to  2018 at various elevations, aspects and soil conditions. A test of  significance in the diameter data collected during the Auditor’s  field visit inferred that the tree growth of Mahogany trees with  the same planting year (e.g. 2010) significantly different at  three different elevations at 5% significance level. The farmers  and WOS staff have also acknowledged the slower growth of  Mahogany trees in the high elevation than in the low elevation.  For this reason, the farmers are demanding a fast growing tree  species on the high elevation.  A CAR 02/19 was raised on this issue seeking an explanation  and justification on how the Project meets the requirement for  identifying the MUs with homogenous characteristics as per the  requirement in 200-GS4GG-Land-Use-Forests-Activity- Requirements-v1.1 on page 3 and pages 28-29.  In response to the CAR 02/19, the Project explained and  justified the current stratification strategy and approach and also  advised that the Project will adapt the RFID technology and  install the system in all trees in between June – October 2019  for accounting CO2 Fixation in each tree in the Project Area. The  Auditors agree that the Project does not need stratification if all  trees are measured and accounted for carbon sequestration.  However, we have a concern that the technology may not be  implemented to the entire Project Area within the next  verification.   FAR 01/19 is created to ensure that the Project has identified  Modelling Units to comply with the requirement as set out in the  GS4GG A/R methodology. If the Project has not rolled out RFID  technology to all trees in the Project Area, the Project must  analyse for the significance difference in the tree growth rates at  different elevations and site conditions (cropland vs grassland)  and appropriately stratify the Project Area into Modelling Units.: CAR 02/19 is closed and FAR 01/19 is created.

A test of  significance in the diameter data collected during the Auditor’s  field visit inferred that the tree growth of Mahogany trees with  the same planting year (e.g. 2010) significantly different at  three different elevations at 5% significance level. The farmers  and WOS staff have also acknowledged the slower growth of  Mahogany trees in the high elevation than in the low elevation.  For this reason, the farmers are demanding a fast growing tree  species on the high elevation.  A CAR 02/19 was raised on this issue seeking an explanation  and justification on how the Project meets the requirement for  identifying the MUs with homogenous characteristics as per the  requirement in 200-GS4GG-Land-Use-Forests-Activity- Requirements-v1.1 on page 3 and pages 28-29.  In response to the CAR 02/19, the Project explained and  justified the current stratification strategy and approach and also  advised that the Project will adapt the RFID technology and  install the system in all trees in between June – October 2019  for accounting CO2 Fixation in each tree in the Project Area. The  Auditors agree that the Project does not need stratification if all  trees are measured and accounted for carbon sequestration.  However, we have a concern that the technology may not be  implemented to the entire Project Area within the next  verification.   FAR 01/19 is created to ensure that the Project has identified  Modelling Units to comply with the requirement as set out in the  GS4GG A/R methodology. If the Project has not rolled out RFID  technology to all trees in the Project Area, the Project must  analyse for the significance difference in the tree growth rates at  different elevations and site conditions (cropland vs grassland)  and appropriately stratify the Project Area into Modelling Units.: Yes                      No

In order to accurately calculate soil and baseline carbon stocks, the project area was

CAR #:: In order to accurately calculate soil and baseline carbon stocks, the project area was  firstly stratified into two modeling units (MUs) according to land use type, i.e.  grassland and croplands. The GS recommended a soil dataset to assess the need to  stratify based on soil type, which was employed (refer to 401.13-AR-T-Baseline  March 2019.pdf). The assessment showed no variation in major soil type across the  project area and as such, further stratification based on soil type was not conducted.    Currently, CO2 Fixation estimates are based on one linear growth rate per species,  derived from the average diameter estimate for each tree age for trees planted  across the two MUs and a range of elevations between 2010 to 2018. The  measurements account for 14% of the total trees planted to date (refer to GS4210  Growth Model to 2018 v1.0.xlsx).  This has been conducted for Mahogany only at  present.  For each monitoring event, the standard deviation was calculated and was  observed to be up to 4 cm variation for a given tree age (note as the auditor  observed, likely to be due to elevation).  The standard error derived (using 90%  confidence interval) did not exceed 3%, except for measurements for the 2010 trees  where the sample size was low. In addition, the relationship of average diameter  with tree age (which accommodates the variation) showed an R2 value of 0.99,  which is exceptionally high. The average growth rates were also compared between  trees measured in croplands and those measured in grasslands and there was  minimal observed variation in the growth rates between grassland and croplands  (refer to 6.1 - Template - Carbon Performance_April 2019.pdf). Therefore, it was | CAR 02/19:

Furthermore,  VVB  confirms  the  baseline  grassland  during  on-site  the  inspection/interviews/i-xxxi/.  For  additional area of trees planted per  year,  the  baseline  carbon  stock  of  croplands  and  grasslands  is  estimated  for  each  additional  area  planted. Risk Area 1: Audit Findings | The Project has changed the Project Area in the reporting period  between 1 March 2017 to 28 Feb 2019.: The review of the Project documents, maps and interview with  the Project Developer, WithOneSeed (WOS) staff and the  participating farmers confirmed that the Project Area has not  experienced any damage due to natural disturbance such as fire  or wind in the reporting period.  The Auditor did not observe any damage or loss of tree  plantation in the Project Area during the site visit to the farmer’s  land. In addition, the GIS files (Land_Use_Cropland.shp;  Land_Use_Forest.shp; Land_Use_Grasslands.shp) showed no  change in the Project Area.  The review of the documentation, GIS files and the interview  with the PD, the participating farmers, and WOS staff concur  that the project area has not changed since the last verification.

Risk Area 10: Audit  Findings | The Project has not accurately accounted soil carbon by the  Project as per the GS4GG requirement.: The Project has used the GS A/R Soil Carbon Guideline and has  not changed the method for estimating soil carbon in the Project  Area since the last verification (24 March 2017) and performance  review. Therefore, the Auditor accepts the method applied to the  Project and did not review the method in this verification.

VB has furthermore verified the screenshots of the evidences and confirms that that error is an oversight  during the design certification and the previous performance certification.  urthermore, VVB would likely to confirm the concern regarding project area has been raised by GSF pre- feasibility  review  and  the  same  has  been  resolved  by  PD  and  GSF  (dated  23.09.2016)  as  stated  under  Response of Project Owner in CAR 5 “Project area is the totality of Baguia and planting area is determined  by the participation of the farmers. The  Auditors agree that the Project does not need stratification if all  trees are measured and accounted for carbon sequestration.  However, we have a concern that the technology may not be  implemented to the entire Project Area within the next  verification.   FAR 01/19 is created to ensure that the Project has identified  Modelling Units to comply with the requirement as set out in the  GS4GG A/R methodology. If the Project has not rolled out RFID  technology to all trees in the Project Area, the Project must  analyse for the significance difference in the tree growth rates at  different elevations and site conditions (cropland vs grassland)  and appropriately stratify the Project Area into Modelling Units.: CAR 02/19 is closed and FAR 01/19 is created.

It is the expectation that the project’s reforestation activities will reduce sub-soil salinity, soil 
erosion and overall improve water quality throughout the project area where the planting activities 
occur.
The auditor finds no reason to believe otherwise.
Reforestation activities on the often steep 
terrain found within the project area will help to stabilize soils and return nutrients to the degraded 
soils.

CAR #:: In order to accurately calculate soil and baseline carbon stocks, the project area was  firstly stratified into two modeling units (MUs) according to land use type, i.e.  grassland and croplands. The GS recommended a soil dataset to assess the need to  stratify based on soil type, which was employed (refer to 401.13-AR-T-Baseline  March 2019.pdf). The assessment showed no variation in major soil type across the  project area and as such, further stratification based on soil type was not conducted.    Currently, CO2 Fixation estimates are based on one linear growth rate per species,  derived from the average diameter estimate for each tree age for trees planted  across the two MUs and a range of elevations between 2010 to 2018. The  measurements account for 14% of the total trees planted to date (refer to GS4210  Growth Model to 2018 v1.0.xlsx).  This has been conducted for Mahogany only at  present.  For each monitoring event, the standard deviation was calculated and was  observed to be up to 4 cm variation for a given tree age (note as the auditor  observed, likely to be due to elevation).  The standard error derived (using 90%  confidence interval) did not exceed 3%, except for measurements for the 2010 trees  where the sample size was low. In addition, the relationship of average diameter  with tree age (which accommodates the variation) showed an R2 value of 0.99,  which is exceptionally high. The average growth rates were also compared between  trees measured in croplands and those measured in grasslands and there was  minimal observed variation in the growth rates between grassland and croplands  (refer to 6.1 - Template - Carbon Performance_April 2019.pdf). 2. Zero hunger.summary: The evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable, Change in farmer income as a result of project, by gender and indigenous status. The context mentions that the incentive payment can double the annual income of a subsistence farmer, which is a significant increase. However, the total cash payment received by participating farmers since the start of the plantation in 2010 is US $150,000, and the annual total payment distributed to participating farmers is approximately ~$83,000. Additionally, the project has contributed over USD$293,437 into the local community since the start of the WithOneSeed program in 2010, and US$474,492 of farmer payments and USD$156,349 in community project funds paid into the Baguia community economy since the GS certification in 2016. These numbers do not directly measure the indicator variable, so the score is 0.

CAR #:: In order to accurately calculate soil and baseline carbon stocks, the project area was  firstly stratified into two modeling units (MUs) according to land use type, i.e.  grassland and croplands. The GS recommended a soil dataset to assess the need to  stratify based on soil type, which was employed (refer to 401.13-AR-T-Baseline  March 2019.pdf). The assessment showed no variation in major soil type across the  project area and as such, further stratification based on soil type was not conducted.    Currently, CO2 Fixation estimates are based on one linear growth rate per species,  derived from the average diameter estimate for each tree age for trees planted  across the two MUs and a range of elevations between 2010 to 2018. The  measurements account for 14% of the total trees planted to date (refer to GS4210  Growth Model to 2018 v1.0.xlsx).  This has been conducted for Mahogany only at  present.  For each monitoring event, the standard deviation was calculated and was  observed to be up to 4 cm variation for a given tree age (note as the auditor  observed, likely to be due to elevation).  The standard error derived (using 90%  confidence interval) did not exceed 3%, except for measurements for the 2010 trees  where the sample size was low. In addition, the relationship of average diameter  with tree age (which accommodates the variation) showed an R2 value of 0.99,  which is exceptionally high. The average growth rates were also compared between  trees measured in croplands and those measured in grasslands and there was  minimal observed variation in the growth rates between grassland and croplands  (refer to 6.1 - Template - Carbon Performance_April 2019.pdf). : Conformance | for estimating the carbon stock.   The Project has tree plantations with age cohorts from 2010 to  2018 at various elevations, aspects and soil conditions. A test of  significance in the diameter data collected during the Auditor’s  field visit inferred that the tree growth of Mahogany trees with  the same planting year (e.g. 2010) significantly different at  three different elevations at 5% significance level. The farmers  and WOS staff have also acknowledged the slower growth of  Mahogany trees in the high elevation than in the low elevation.  For this reason, the farmers are demanding a fast growing tree  species on the high elevation.  A CAR 02/19 was raised on this issue seeking an explanation  and justification on how the Project meets the requirement for  identifying the MUs with homogenous characteristics as per the  requirement in 200-GS4GG-Land-Use-Forests-Activity- Requirements-v1.1 on page 3 and pages 28-29.  In response to the CAR 02/19, the Project explained and  justified the current stratification strategy and approach and also  advised that the Project will adapt the RFID technology and  install the system in all trees in between June – October 2019  for accounting CO2 Fixation in each tree in the Project Area. The  Auditors agree that the Project does not need stratification if all  trees are measured and accounted for carbon sequestration.  However, we have a concern that the technology may not be  implemented to the entire Project Area within the next  verification.   FAR 01/19 is created to ensure that the Project has identified  Modelling Units to comply with the requirement as set out in the  GS4GG A/R methodology. If the Project has not rolled out RFID  technology to all trees in the Project Area, the Project must  analyse for the significance difference in the tree growth rates at  different elevations and site conditions (cropland vs grassland)  and appropriately stratify the Project Area into Modelling Units.: Yes                      No

: Relevant  CAR/FAR/OBS | for estimating the carbon stock.   The Project has tree plantations with age cohorts from 2010 to  2018 at various elevations, aspects and soil conditions. A test of  significance in the diameter data collected during the Auditor’s  field visit inferred that the tree growth of Mahogany trees with  the same planting year (e.g. 2010) significantly different at  three different elevations at 5% significance level. The farmers  and WOS staff have also acknowledged the slower growth of  Mahogany trees in the high elevation than in the low elevation.  For this reason, the farmers are demanding a fast growing tree  species on the high elevation.  A CAR 02/19 was raised on this issue seeking an explanation  and justification on how the Project meets the requirement for  identifying the MUs with homogenous characteristics as per the  requirement in 200-GS4GG-Land-Use-Forests-Activity- Requirements-v1.1 on page 3 and pages 28-29.  In response to the CAR 02/19, the Project explained and  justified the current stratification strategy and approach and also  advised that the Project will adapt the RFID technology and  install the system in all trees in between June – October 2019  for accounting CO2 Fixation in each tree in the Project Area. The  Auditors agree that the Project does not need stratification if all  trees are measured and accounted for carbon sequestration.  However, we have a concern that the technology may not be  implemented to the entire Project Area within the next  verification.   FAR 01/19 is created to ensure that the Project has identified  Modelling Units to comply with the requirement as set out in the  GS4GG A/R methodology. If the Project has not rolled out RFID  technology to all trees in the Project Area, the Project must  analyse for the significance difference in the tree growth rates at  different elevations and site conditions (cropland vs grassland)  and appropriately stratify the Project Area into Modelling Units.: CAR 02/19 and FAR 01/19

Risk Area 2: Audit Findings | A risk that the Project has not accurately mapped and estimated  the Planting Area.: The Planting Area (PA) represents the land area where the  participating farmers have customary and undisputed land rights  and have undertaken tree plantation activities since 2010. The  PA is derived by applying a buffer area of 5 m diameter around  each planted tree, representing an average width of tree crown  at maturity. The cumulative Planting Area was obtained by  merging the tree buffer area around each tree.   The interview with the stakeholders did not raise any issues on  the exclusion of areas of special interest such as spiritual,  cultural and social significance. However, the farmers raised an  issue on planting on the disputed land due to lack of a clear  recognition of customary right to a farmer. The WOS staff  clarified to the farmers that the Project does not accept  plantation on disputed land and further highlighted that the  plantation must occur only on land with a clear customary land  right.  The plantation activity must be confined to the Project Area in  the Cropland and/or Grassland but must not occur on the  baseline Forestland. However, the Intersection Tool in the GIS  located overlap between Cumulative EPA 2017 (Cumulative EPA  2017.shp) and Forest land (Land_Use_Forest.shp) maps at 102  locations (Refer to Image 1). Principle 1: Contribution to Climate Security & Sustainable Development 
The project is an eligible project type in the Land Use and Forestry (LUF) sector of the GSF Program.
The project activities are Afforestation/Reforestation (AR) of native and exotic non-invasive tree 
species on degraded cropland and grasslands by smallholder farmers in Timor Leste.
The Project’s 
AR activities are implemented according to the GS4GG Land Use and Forestry (LUF) Activity

200-GS4GG-Land-Use-Forests-Activity- Requirements-v1.1, page 4  Reference:  GS4GG A/R Methodology, condition 2 of Scope and  Applicability: This CAR 08/19 is created because CAR 01/19 was not resolved in the first round of  ‘List of uncorrected errors’ issued on 9 April 2019. Please refer to CAR 01/19 for the  detail to this CAR.  The Auditors acknowledge the PD’s response to CAR 01/19. The Eligible Planting  Area (EPA) and the adjacent areas of Forestland were defined in the Project Design.  These areas were validated and the areas defined have not been changed since the  project validation since the project start in 2010. According to condition 2 of Scope  and Applicability in the GS4GG A/R Methodology, "Projects that include the planting  of trees on land that does not meet the definition of a forest at planting start are  eligible to apply this methodology.”  These seedlings were planted on land that was  defined as forestland and was not part of the Eligible Planting Area at the project  start.   We requested a clarification from the Gold Standard Secretariat on this issue (email  sent on 28 April 2019 to Giancarlo Raschio). On 8 May 2019, the email from the GS  advised that ‘a project cannot claim carbon credits from new plantations beyond the  planting area at validation unless there is a new area certification process’. (The  email from GS was shared with the PD). Therefore, the Project must exclude the  planting area on the forestland from the Project Area and update the planted area  map (Cumulative EPA 2017.shp; Cumulative EPA 2016.shp) provided for  verification.

Good health and well being.summary: The evidence from the WithOneSeed_2021_GSF_PerformCert_Report_v1.3_20211006 (Page 31) mentions that agroforestry practices help alleviate food scarcity issues and improve soil quality, which is a qualitative statement (Score = 1). The project's reforestation activities reduce sub-soil salinity, soil erosion, and improve water quality, which is also a qualitative statement (Score = 1). The project's ability to provide a financial return to farmers through the sale of carbon credits incentivizes them to adopt sustainable practices, which is a qualitative statement (Score = 1). Furthermore, the project's focus on soil stabilization and nutrient return to degraded soils supports the adoption of alternative measures, which is a qualitative statement (Score = 1). However, none of the evidence explicitly mentions the number of farmers who have adopted alternative measures or reduced/avoided open burning of biomass and crop residue, which is the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable.

Gender equality.summary: No supporting evidence was found in the provided context to determine the average time saving (in minutes/hr per household) associated with cooking time and fuel collection due to the adoption of project technology. The context primarily focuses on the project's carbon sequestration, financial additionality, and socio-economic benefits, but does not provide information on the time spent on cooking and fuel collection by households. The only relevant information found is that nearly a third of the households across the project area support and are supported by the project, but this information does not provide demographic disaggregation or time saving data. Therefore, the evidence fails to satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

VB has furthermore verified the screenshots of the evidences and confirms that that error is an oversight  during the design certification and the previous performance certification.  urthermore, VVB would likely to confirm the concern regarding project area has been raised by GSF pre- feasibility  review  and  the  same  has  been  resolved  by  PD  and  GSF  (dated  23.09.2016)  as  stated  under  Response of Project Owner in CAR 5 “Project area is the totality of Baguia and planting area is determined  by the participation of the farmers. :  | Please see the figure below for better understanding and reference of shapefiles  of this folder.: T hrough  on-site  inspection  interviews,  VVB  confirms  that  PD  has  submitted  these same shapefiles during previous audits and has been validated & verified  by previous auditors and Gold Standard. Clean water and sanitation.summary: The evidence from the WithOneSeed_2021_GSF_PerformCert_Report_v1.3_20211006 document does not directly measure the turbidity of river water, which is the SDG indicator. Although the auditor analyzed spatial datasets and assessed them in GIS software (Document: WithOneSeed_2021_GSF_PerformCert_Report_v1.3_20211006, Page: 36), no specific information on turbidity trends or changes is mentioned. Therefore, the evidence is qualitative only and does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

6. Clean water and sanitation.summary: The evidence from WithOneSeed_2021_GSF_PerformCert_Report_v1.3_20211006, Page 29, mentions the project's potential positive impact on water quality and quantity, but does not explicitly measure the capacity of water systems to retain and manage water resources sustainably. Although the auditor found no evidence of negative impact, this does not directly support the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence is qualitative and does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

PRINCIPLE 9.2: VULNERABILITY TO NATURAL DISASTER 
The Proponent acknowledges that seasonal flooding and other climate related events may impact 
Timor Leste and the communities of Baguia when they occur.
It is the expectation however, that the 
project’s reforestation activities will reduce sub-soil salinity, soil erosion and overall improve water 
quality and soil stability throughout the project area and will therefore reduce the impact from such 
natural disturbances.

The 
auditor is reasonably assured that the project does not affect the availability or reliability of energy 
supply within the local communities.

areas, and baseline management units, the Proponent also identified and mapped the major water 
courses within the project area.

All Projects must demonstrate impacts from the project activities are additional when compared to 
the baseline scenario to show that the benefits generated by the project are beyond the business-
as-usual scenario.

Since year 2016 in the life of the project, the emission reduction estimates and 
associated CO2 Certificate calculations are based on the total tree counts, by species 
multiplied by the species specific sequestration rates estimated by the Proponent 
based on their annual forest inventory sampling (measurements).
This results the 
emission reductions being estimated based on sequestration at the tree level, which

and the resulting income has allowed the project to begin transitioning towards financial self-
sustainability by 2021.
From the outset the Proponent has been committed to paying the 
participating smallholder subsistence farmers an annual incentive payment for each tree they 
maintain.
The program has maintained this commitment for the past 10 years.

direct payments to farmers for planting and maintaining project trees has contributed over 
$500,000 into the communities of Baguia since the Project’s initial certification under the GSF 
program in 2016.

The WOS staff, farmers and the local representative of the Government strongly

7. Affordable and clean energy.summary: The evidence from the WithOneSeed_2021_GSF_PerformCert_Report_v1.3_20211006 document does not directly measure the indicator variable. Although it mentions the project's focus on forestry carbon sequestration and the sale of high-quality Gold Standard certified carbon credits, this information is indirect and does not provide explicit quantitative data on the number of individuals provided access to clean fuels and technologies. The document also states that the project activities do not involve significant electrical power uses, except for the HMI office buildings, but this is not directly related to the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence is treated as qualitative (Score = 1).

As a Registered Greenhouse and Energy Auditor

energy audit and assurance engagements under the Australian NGERS, CFI, EEO, 
and NCOS, and the international Carbon Disclosure Project (CDP), conducting 
energy audits, undertaking environmental risk reviews under EPA Regulations 
and environmental license-to-operate.

Sam Phua, Audit Team Member: Sam is an environment, energy and carbon services consultant with extensive  greenhouse gas emissions and energy assurance and advisory experience across  a wide range of industries. As a Registered Greenhouse and Energy Auditor  (Category 1 Technical, Category 1 Non-Technical and Category 2) with the  Australian Clean Energy Regulator, his areas of expertise include greenhouse and  energy audit and assurance engagements under the Australian NGERS, CFI, EEO,  and NCOS, and the international Carbon Disclosure Project (CDP), conducting  energy audits, undertaking environmental risk reviews under EPA Regulations  and environmental license-to-operate. Sam is also a Registered Chartered  Chemical Engineer (CEng, MIChemE) and Registered Chartered Professional  Engineer (CPEng, MIEAust, NER).  Sam is also Australia’s first and only Approved Verifier for the international  Airport Carbon Accreditation. Sam also sits on the Clean Energy Regulator’s  Carbon Farming Initiative (CFI) technical expert panel.  Sam is the lead author on one of the world’s first Improved Forest Management- Logged to Protected Forests (IFM-LtPF) Methodology under the Verified Carbon  Standard (VCS). This Methodology has been approved for use and has been  assigned a VCS Methodology reference of VM0011. Sam has also authored  several reviews for methodologies and Project Design Descriptions (PDDs)  seeking approval under the VCS, Clean Development Mechanism (CDM) and the  Climate, Community and Biodiversity Standard (CCBS). Sam is also proficient in  Australia’s National Carbon Accounting Toolbox (NCAT) having previously  undertaken formal training in the use and application of the fullCAM model. He  provides advice to stakeholders on (1) forestry carbon sequestration projects

ensuring access to services for those who have little access, and enabling /building
community 
empowerment.

policies/approaches that include rules about ensuring human rights are realized, the rule of law is

is focused on maintaining safe and satisfactory employment conditions.

CAR #:: In order to accurately calculate soil and baseline carbon stocks, the project area was  firstly stratified into two modeling units (MUs) according to land use type, i.e.  grassland and croplands. The GS recommended a soil dataset to assess the need to  stratify based on soil type, which was employed (refer to 401.13-AR-T-Baseline  March 2019.pdf). The assessment showed no variation in major soil type across the  project area and as such, further stratification based on soil type was not conducted.    Currently, CO2 Fixation estimates are based on one linear growth rate per species,  derived from the average diameter estimate for each tree age for trees planted  across the two MUs and a range of elevations between 2010 to 2018. The  measurements account for 14% of the total trees planted to date (refer to GS4210  Growth Model to 2018 v1.0.xlsx).  This has been conducted for Mahogany only at  present.  For each monitoring event, the standard deviation was calculated and was  observed to be up to 4 cm variation for a given tree age (note as the auditor  observed, likely to be due to elevation).  The standard error derived (using 90%  confidence interval) did not exceed 3%, except for measurements for the 2010 trees  where the sample size was low. In addition, the relationship of average diameter  with tree age (which accommodates the variation) showed an R2 value of 0.99,  which is exceptionally high. The average growth rates were also compared between  trees measured in croplands and those measured in grasslands and there was  minimal observed variation in the growth rates between grassland and croplands  (refer to 6.1 - Template - Carbon Performance_April 2019.pdf). Therefore, it was | CAR 02/19:

Risk Area 3: Audit Findings | The Project has not accurately identified the Modelling Unit (MU)  in the Project Area based on the GS4GG requirements.: The Project has identified two Modelling Units in the Project Area  based on the baseline land use, such as grassland and cropland.  The Project has not changed stratification since the last  Verification and Performance Review.   According to the GS4GG Requirement (200-GS4GG-Land-Use- Forests-Activity-Requirements-v1.1 on page 4-5), a modelling  unit should comprise an area with homogenous characteristics in  terms of growth, species, management regime and planting  year.  Although the Project has two MUs based on the baseline  condition prior to the tree planting, the same growth rate was  applied to both MUs derived by combining all the tree  measurement data. Despite identifying two modelling units with  variability in terms of planting year, elevation and site  conditions, the Project did not differentiate between the two MUs

Life on land.summary: The evidence from the WithOneSeed_2021_GSF_PerformCert_Report_v1.3_20211006 document (Page 42) mentions the project's aim to reforest degraded croplands and grasslands, contributing to sustainable forest management. However, this is a qualitative statement and does not directly measure the indicator variable. The project's compensation structure discourages participating farmers from harvesting trees, and the project has resulted in a continual increase of the number of trees under its management (Document: WithOneSeed_2021_GSF_PerformCert_Report_v1.3_20211006%281%29.pdf, Page: 45), which is also qualitative. The project's emission reduction estimates are based on sequestration at the tree level, with a total of 32,094 tCO2e in emission reductions for the third Monitoring Period (Document: WithOneSeed_2021_GSF_PerformCert_Report_v1.3_20211006%281%29.pdf, Page: 61), but this is not directly related to non-renewable wood fuel consumption. Therefore, the evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable.

Risk Area 2: Audit Findings | A risk that the Project has not accurately mapped and estimated  the Planting Area.: The Planting Area (PA) represents the land area where the  participating farmers have customary and undisputed land rights  and have undertaken tree plantation activities since 2010. The  PA is derived by applying a buffer area of 5 m diameter around  each planted tree, representing an average width of tree crown  at maturity. The cumulative Planting Area was obtained by  merging the tree buffer area around each tree.   The interview with the stakeholders did not raise any issues on  the exclusion of areas of special interest such as spiritual,  cultural and social significance. However, the farmers raised an  issue on planting on the disputed land due to lack of a clear  recognition of customary right to a farmer. The WOS staff  clarified to the farmers that the Project does not accept  plantation on disputed land and further highlighted that the  plantation must occur only on land with a clear customary land  right.  The plantation activity must be confined to the Project Area in  the Cropland and/or Grassland but must not occur on the  baseline Forestland. However, the Intersection Tool in the GIS  located overlap between Cumulative EPA 2017 (Cumulative EPA  2017.shp) and Forest land (Land_Use_Forest.shp) maps at 102  locations (Refer to Image 1). Project Owner's response:: The requirement to not plant on baseline forestland derives from the Gold  Standard’s Additionality requirement for A/R projects, which states under the No  deforestation section:   “The project area shall not have been forest for at least 10 years prior to the  planting start, OR  In case the project area has been deforested during the last 10 years, evidence shall  be given that the deforestation activity has not taken place with the intention to  reforest the area and generate CO2-certificates.”   Much of the forest that remains in Baguia is degraded natural forest that covers the  landscape in a sparse mosaic pattern, as demonstrated during the site visit.  All of  the land on which this forest mosaic remains is small land holder  owned.  WithOneSeed sees the reforesting of this land as integral to the community  forestry model.  The baseline forest cover map for the project area (e.g. subdistrict of Baguia) is:  Land_use_Forest.shp and this map was prepared on the basis of 2010 plantings and  hence Landsat imagery from 1999 to 2009, e.g. 10 years to the commencement of  the initial planting.  For 2016 – the baseline period would be 2006 - 2015 and for  2017, it would be 2007 – 2016. As new plantings have been made and will be made  for each year and new trees will continue to be planted, it is not practical to revise  the baseline forest cover map accordingly and as such the EPAs from the  subsequent years (e.g. 2016 and 2017) may overlap.   However, in addition, the resolution of Landsat is approximately 25 to 30m pixel  size, whilst the EPA areas have a resolution of 5m. Farmers are planting on their  plot of land which area dispersed in between forested areas. Therefore, forest land is  likely to not be as accurate as the EPAs and as the planting is inter dispersed  between the forestland, this may also lead to overlap. The accuracy of the GPS  readings cannot always be guaranteed. Needless to say, farmers cant plant trees on  trees.  The figure below presents changes in carbon stocks across the subdistrict of Baguia  using FlintPro Software1. FlintPro Software is based on Landsat imagery provided  USGS and presented in the WGS84/NUTM projection. Further information in regard  to Flint Pro can be provided upon request.  The results show that since near the  project commencement, 2011, above and below ground carbon stocks have been  increase and atmosphere carbon has been decreasing.  As carbon in newly planted  trees cannot compensate for the loss of mature forest, this clearly suggests that  there has been little to negligible deforestation across the subdistrict of Baguia since  2011.  Before this period, according to a recent Asian Development Bank (ADB)  report2: “Timor-Leste has experienced massive deforestation; estimates of clearing  of original forest cover are as high as 90%. Most land shows evidence of clearing,  especially slash-and-burn cultivation, harvesting of firewood, coffee growing, and  grazing, even on steep slopes. Forest cover has steadily declined since the 1970s  and the current deforestation rate is thought to be about 1.3% per annum. A major  driver is conversion of forest to agricultural land.”   There is no evidence that any of  the deforestation in Timor-Leste is the result of clearing land for the purpose of  replanting trees for C02 sequestration purposes.  As suggested by the ADB report  agriculture, firewood harvesting and other timber needs are the main driver for land

CAR #:: In order to accurately calculate soil and baseline carbon stocks, the project area was  firstly stratified into two modeling units (MUs) according to land use type, i.e.  grassland and croplands. The GS recommended a soil dataset to assess the need to  stratify based on soil type, which was employed (refer to 401.13-AR-T-Baseline  March 2019.pdf). The assessment showed no variation in major soil type across the  project area and as such, further stratification based on soil type was not conducted.    Currently, CO2 Fixation estimates are based on one linear growth rate per species,  derived from the average diameter estimate for each tree age for trees planted  across the two MUs and a range of elevations between 2010 to 2018. The  measurements account for 14% of the total trees planted to date (refer to GS4210  Growth Model to 2018 v1.0.xlsx).  This has been conducted for Mahogany only at  present.  For each monitoring event, the standard deviation was calculated and was  observed to be up to 4 cm variation for a given tree age (note as the auditor  observed, likely to be due to elevation).  The standard error derived (using 90%  confidence interval) did not exceed 3%, except for measurements for the 2010 trees  where the sample size was low. In addition, the relationship of average diameter  with tree age (which accommodates the variation) showed an R2 value of 0.99,  which is exceptionally high. The average growth rates were also compared between  trees measured in croplands and those measured in grasslands and there was  minimal observed variation in the growth rates between grassland and croplands  (refer to 6.1 - Template - Carbon Performance_April 2019.pdf). Therefore, it was | CAR 02/19:

Project Owner's response:: The requirement to not plant on baseline forestland derives from the Gold  Standard’s Additionality requirement for A/R projects, which states under the No  deforestation section:   “The project area shall not have been forest for at least 10 years prior to the  planting start, OR  In case the project area has been deforested during the last 10 years, evidence shall  be given that the deforestation activity has not taken place with the intention to  reforest the area and generate CO2-certificates.”   Much of the forest that remains in Baguia is degraded natural forest that covers the  landscape in a sparse mosaic pattern, as demonstrated during the site visit.  All of  the land on which this forest mosaic remains is small land holder  owned.  WithOneSeed sees the reforesting of this land as integral to the community  forestry model.  The baseline forest cover map for the project area (e.g. subdistrict of Baguia) is:  Land_use_Forest.shp and this map was prepared on the basis of 2010 plantings and  hence Landsat imagery from 1999 to 2009, e.g. 10 years to the commencement of  the initial planting.  For 2016 – the baseline period would be 2006 - 2015 and for  2017, it would be 2007 – 2016. As new plantings have been made and will be made  for each year and new trees will continue to be planted, it is not practical to revise  the baseline forest cover map accordingly and as such the EPAs from the  subsequent years (e.g. 2016 and 2017) may overlap.   However, in addition, the resolution of Landsat is approximately 25 to 30m pixel  size, whilst the EPA areas have a resolution of 5m. Farmers are planting on their  plot of land which area dispersed in between forested areas. Therefore, forest land is  likely to not be as accurate as the EPAs and as the planting is inter dispersed  between the forestland, this may also lead to overlap. The accuracy of the GPS  readings cannot always be guaranteed. Needless to say, farmers cant plant trees on  trees.  The figure below presents changes in carbon stocks across the subdistrict of Baguia  using FlintPro Software1. FlintPro Software is based on Landsat imagery provided  USGS and presented in the WGS84/NUTM projection. Further information in regard  to Flint Pro can be provided upon request.  The results show that since near the  project commencement, 2011, above and below ground carbon stocks have been  increase and atmosphere carbon has been decreasing.  As carbon in newly planted  trees cannot compensate for the loss of mature forest, this clearly suggests that  there has been little to negligible deforestation across the subdistrict of Baguia since  2011.  Before this period, according to a recent Asian Development Bank (ADB)  report2: “Timor-Leste has experienced massive deforestation; estimates of clearing  of original forest cover are as high as 90%. Most land shows evidence of clearing,  especially slash-and-burn cultivation, harvesting of firewood, coffee growing, and  grazing, even on steep slopes. Forest cover has steadily declined since the 1970s  and the current deforestation rate is thought to be about 1.3% per annum. A major  driver is conversion of forest to agricultural land.”   There is no evidence that any of  the deforestation in Timor-Leste is the result of clearing land for the purpose of  replanting trees for C02 sequestration purposes.  As suggested by the ADB report  agriculture, firewood harvesting and other timber needs are the main driver for land

: Audit  Findings | accurately for estimating carbon fixation for the project.: The Project had appropriately selected aboveground biomass,  belowground biomass pool and soil carbon for accounting carbon  sequestration by an afforestation and reforestation program.  The Project had measured the circumference of tree at the breast  height (1.3 m) and also recorded the GPS of each measured tree.  The PD has not changed the method for estimating aboveground  biomass in the Project Area since the last verification (24 March  2017) and performance review. Therefore, the Auditor accepts  the method applied to the Project and did not review the method  in this verification.  For belowground biomass, the project used the same value of  root:shoot ratio of 0.2 (Gold Standard Default) as applied for  Performance Certification in 2017.   The Project calculated the standard error for the plantation years  2010-2017 based on the tree measurement data and found a  maximum error below 7% i.e. well below the threshold of 20%.  In response to CAR 06/19, the Project updated the CO2 Fixation  Calculations, which estimated 24,320 tCO2 in this verification  period (2017 & 2018) after subtracting the overestimated 3,166  tCO2 carbon fixation in year 2016 at the previous verification and  performance review.

15. Life on land.indicator: Total above and below ground biomass stock in forest

15. Life on land.summary: The evidence provided does not directly measure the total area of trees planted. The cumulative crown area of trees planted between 2010 to 2022 was estimated at 348.4 ha (Document: GS4210_Final-Monitoring-Report+07+May+2024.pdf, Page: 9), but this figure is not explicitly stated as the total area of trees planted. The cumulative crown area of trees planted was estimated at 472.1 hectares in 2022, but this figure is considered inaccurate (Document: CCIPL+1481%28A%29+_Final+Performance+Certification+Report+-CC.pdf, Page: 66). Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

The
planted area is determined based on delineating the area of tree plantings on all the
farms involved in the project from the GPS coordinates of every tree planted and the
projected crown diameter of the mature tree species.
For plantings conducted between 2010 to 2022, the cumulative crown area of the
trees planted was estimated at 348.4 ha.
Staff used RFID tags and the TreeO2 app to identify, scan and count planted trees on
an annual basis.

Risk Area 2: Audit Findings | A risk that the Project has not accurately mapped and estimated  the Planting Area.: The Planting Area (PA) represents the land area where the  participating farmers have customary and undisputed land rights  and have undertaken tree plantation activities since 2010. The  PA is derived by applying a buffer area of 5 m diameter around  each planted tree, representing an average width of tree crown  at maturity. The cumulative Planting Area was obtained by  merging the tree buffer area around each tree.   The interview with the stakeholders did not raise any issues on  the exclusion of areas of special interest such as spiritual,  cultural and social significance. However, the farmers raised an  issue on planting on the disputed land due to lack of a clear  recognition of customary right to a farmer. The WOS staff  clarified to the farmers that the Project does not accept  plantation on disputed land and further highlighted that the  plantation must occur only on land with a clear customary land  right.  The plantation activity must be confined to the Project Area in  the Cropland and/or Grassland but must not occur on the  baseline Forestland. However, the Intersection Tool in the GIS  located overlap between Cumulative EPA 2017 (Cumulative EPA  2017.shp) and Forest land (Land_Use_Forest.shp) maps at 102  locations (Refer to Image 1). : Relevant  CAR/FAR/OBS | for estimating the carbon stock.   The Project has tree plantations with age cohorts from 2010 to  2018 at various elevations, aspects and soil conditions. A test of  significance in the diameter data collected during the Auditor’s  field visit inferred that the tree growth of Mahogany trees with  the same planting year (e.g. 2010) significantly different at  three different elevations at 5% significance level. The farmers  and WOS staff have also acknowledged the slower growth of  Mahogany trees in the high elevation than in the low elevation.  For this reason, the farmers are demanding a fast growing tree  species on the high elevation.  A CAR 02/19 was raised on this issue seeking an explanation  and justification on how the Project meets the requirement for  identifying the MUs with homogenous characteristics as per the  requirement in 200-GS4GG-Land-Use-Forests-Activity- Requirements-v1.1 on page 3 and pages 28-29.  In response to the CAR 02/19, the Project explained and  justified the current stratification strategy and approach and also  advised that the Project will adapt the RFID technology and  install the system in all trees in between June – October 2019  for accounting CO2 Fixation in each tree in the Project Area. The  Auditors agree that the Project does not need stratification if all  trees are measured and accounted for carbon sequestration.  However, we have a concern that the technology may not be  implemented to the entire Project Area within the next  verification.   FAR 01/19 is created to ensure that the Project has identified  Modelling Units to comply with the requirement as set out in the  GS4GG A/R methodology. If the Project has not rolled out RFID  technology to all trees in the Project Area, the Project must  analyse for the significance difference in the tree growth rates at  different elevations and site conditions (cropland vs grassland)  and appropriately stratify the Project Area into Modelling Units.: CAR 02/19 and FAR 01/19

Means of validation  DR, OSV, I: Conclusion | : T   T   V   P Based on the review of GS MR/02/, supporting documents and further during  the on-site inspection/interviews/i-xxxi/, the project stratification is done based  on  the  number  of  Baguia  farmers  i.e.,  922  Baguia  farmers  are  engaged  in  community forestry under this project and a total of 406,071 trees have been  planted under management on private smallholder farms.  he  plantations  have  been  done  through  reforestation  with  native  or  naturalised  tree  species:  Swietenia  Macrophylla  (Mahogany),  Eucalyptus  Urophylla (Mountain Gum), Tectona Grandis (Teak), Casuarina Equisetifolia  (Sheoak),  Dalbergia  Nigra  (Rosewood),  Santalum  album  (Sandalwood),  Sterculia Foetida (Wild almond) and Toona Ciliata (Red Cedar).  he plantations are developed in the nursery following with a rigorous process  of selection and further transplanted in the project site after 3 months. VVB  during the on-site inspection/i-xxxi/ has visited the nursery and has interviewed/i- xxxi/  the  relevant  personnels  involved  in  the  nursery  management  and  operation.  VVB,  in the on-site  inspection/i-xxxi/,  has verified that saplings are  sourced from the local community and subsequently provided to farmers at  no charge. Specifically, species with small or micro seedlings are nurtured in  coco pits until  they reach  maturity for transplantation into polybags.  After a  period  of  three  months,  these  well-developed  saplings  are  distributed  to  farmers for planting in their respective fields.   VB  has  additionally  reviewed  the  Risk  Register/18/  and  other  supporting  documentation  related  to  annual  activities  encompassing  capacity-building  initiatives,  monitoring  protocols,  and  qualifications  in  forestry,  operations,  finance,  legal  aspects,  as  well  as  the  technical  qualifications  of  workers  involved  in  implementation.  The  assessment  also  encompasses  technical  equipment,  financial  safeguards,  and  measures  taken  to  mitigate  risks  associated with drought, flood, hail, snow, heavy rains, hurricanes, domestic  and  wild  threats,  diseases,  frost,  heat,  irregular  resettlement,  or  illicit  crop  production. : Conformance | for estimating the carbon stock.   The Project has tree plantations with age cohorts from 2010 to  2018 at various elevations, aspects and soil conditions. A test of  significance in the diameter data collected during the Auditor’s  field visit inferred that the tree growth of Mahogany trees with  the same planting year (e.g. 2010) significantly different at  three different elevations at 5% significance level. The farmers  and WOS staff have also acknowledged the slower growth of  Mahogany trees in the high elevation than in the low elevation.  For this reason, the farmers are demanding a fast growing tree  species on the high elevation.  A CAR 02/19 was raised on this issue seeking an explanation  and justification on how the Project meets the requirement for  identifying the MUs with homogenous characteristics as per the  requirement in 200-GS4GG-Land-Use-Forests-Activity- Requirements-v1.1 on page 3 and pages 28-29.  In response to the CAR 02/19, the Project explained and  justified the current stratification strategy and approach and also  advised that the Project will adapt the RFID technology and  install the system in all trees in between June – October 2019  for accounting CO2 Fixation in each tree in the Project Area. The  Auditors agree that the Project does not need stratification if all  trees are measured and accounted for carbon sequestration.  However, we have a concern that the technology may not be  implemented to the entire Project Area within the next  verification.   FAR 01/19 is created to ensure that the Project has identified  Modelling Units to comply with the requirement as set out in the  GS4GG A/R methodology. If the Project has not rolled out RFID  technology to all trees in the Project Area, the Project must  analyse for the significance difference in the tree growth rates at  different elevations and site conditions (cropland vs grassland)  and appropriately stratify the Project Area into Modelling Units.: Yes                      No

What is the average annual rate of tree.content: Risk Area 2: Audit Findings | A risk that the Project has not accurately mapped and estimated  the Planting Area.: The Planting Area (PA) represents the land area where the  participating farmers have customary and undisputed land rights  and have undertaken tree plantation activities since 2010. The  PA is derived by applying a buffer area of 5 m diameter around  each planted tree, representing an average width of tree crown  at maturity. The cumulative Planting Area was obtained by  merging the tree buffer area around each tree.   The interview with the stakeholders did not raise any issues on  the exclusion of areas of special interest such as spiritual,  cultural and social significance. However, the farmers raised an  issue on planting on the disputed land due to lack of a clear  recognition of customary right to a farmer. The WOS staff  clarified to the farmers that the Project does not accept  plantation on disputed land and further highlighted that the  plantation must occur only on land with a clear customary land  right.  The plantation activity must be confined to the Project Area in  the Cropland and/or Grassland but must not occur on the  baseline Forestland. However, the Intersection Tool in the GIS  located overlap between Cumulative EPA 2017 (Cumulative EPA  2017.shp) and Forest land (Land_Use_Forest.shp) maps at 102  locations (Refer to Image 1). This satisfies the indicator's measurement requirement. The percentage of the project area that is protected land area is 70.8% (14,508 / 20,578), which is also explicitly stated in the context. This further supports the indicator's measurement requirement.

What percentage of the project area is p.content: Risk Area 2: Audit Findings | A risk that the Project has not accurately mapped and estimated  the Planting Area.: The Planting Area (PA) represents the land area where the  participating farmers have customary and undisputed land rights  and have undertaken tree plantation activities since 2010. The  PA is derived by applying a buffer area of 5 m diameter around  each planted tree, representing an average width of tree crown  at maturity. The cumulative Planting Area was obtained by  merging the tree buffer area around each tree.   The interview with the stakeholders did not raise any issues on  the exclusion of areas of special interest such as spiritual,  cultural and social significance. However, the farmers raised an  issue on planting on the disputed land due to lack of a clear  recognition of customary right to a farmer. The WOS staff  clarified to the farmers that the Project does not accept  plantation on disputed land and further highlighted that the  plantation must occur only on land with a clear customary land  right.  The plantation activity must be confined to the Project Area in  the Cropland and/or Grassland but must not occur on the  baseline Forestland. However, the Intersection Tool in the GIS  located overlap between Cumulative EPA 2017 (Cumulative EPA  2017.shp) and Forest land (Land_Use_Forest.shp) maps at 102  locations (Refer to Image 1). We also noticed that the

Risk Area 1: Audit Findings | The Project has changed the Project Area in the reporting period  between 1 March 2017 to 28 Feb 2019.: The review of the Project documents, maps and interview with  the Project Developer, WithOneSeed (WOS) staff and the  participating farmers confirmed that the Project Area has not  experienced any damage due to natural disturbance such as fire  or wind in the reporting period.  The Auditor did not observe any damage or loss of tree  plantation in the Project Area during the site visit to the farmer’s  land. In addition, the GIS files (Land_Use_Cropland.shp;  Land_Use_Forest.shp; Land_Use_Grasslands.shp) showed no  change in the Project Area.  The review of the documentation, GIS files and the interview  with the PD, the participating farmers, and WOS staff concur  that the project area has not changed since the last verification.

The Proponent’s response confirms that the Cumulative EPA area values (ha) indeed 
are not used to estimate the emission reductions and associated CO2 Certificate 
calculations.
For years 2010 – 2015 of the project, the Cumulative EPA figures were 
used to derive the total number of trees on each MU as a proportion of the total area 
planted on Croplands vs Grasslands.

Again, 
the auditor’s independent calculation of the Cumulative EPA for year 2017 was 
147.67 which is reasonable to that found by the Proponent and what is entered in 
the CO2 Certificate calculation workbook for this year (146.93).

Risk Area 3: Audit Findings | The Project has not accurately identified the Modelling Unit (MU)  in the Project Area based on the GS4GG requirements.: The Project has identified two Modelling Units in the Project Area  based on the baseline land use, such as grassland and cropland.  The Project has not changed stratification since the last  Verification and Performance Review.   According to the GS4GG Requirement (200-GS4GG-Land-Use- Forests-Activity-Requirements-v1.1 on page 4-5), a modelling  unit should comprise an area with homogenous characteristics in  terms of growth, species, management regime and planting  year.  Although the Project has two MUs based on the baseline  condition prior to the tree planting, the same growth rate was  applied to both MUs derived by combining all the tree  measurement data. Despite identifying two modelling units with  variability in terms of planting year, elevation and site  conditions, the Project did not differentiate between the two MUs

15. Life on land.target: 15.5 Protect and prevent extinction of threatened species

15. Life on land.summary: The evidence provided in the Intermediate Answers and Context satisfies the indicator's measurement requirement. The total land area where native species were planted is estimated at 348.4 ha (Document: GS4210_Final-Monitoring-Report+07+May+2024.pdf, Page: 9). This estimate is based on the cumulative crown area of the trees planted between 2010 to 2022. The evidence directly measures the indicator variable, Total Area of Trees Planted: Native Species, and is therefore assigned a Score of 2.

What is the total land area where native.content: Risk Area 2: Audit Findings | A risk that the Project has not accurately mapped and estimated  the Planting Area.: The Planting Area (PA) represents the land area where the  participating farmers have customary and undisputed land rights  and have undertaken tree plantation activities since 2010. The  PA is derived by applying a buffer area of 5 m diameter around  each planted tree, representing an average width of tree crown  at maturity. The cumulative Planting Area was obtained by  merging the tree buffer area around each tree.   The interview with the stakeholders did not raise any issues on  the exclusion of areas of special interest such as spiritual,  cultural and social significance. However, the farmers raised an  issue on planting on the disputed land due to lack of a clear  recognition of customary right to a farmer. The WOS staff  clarified to the farmers that the Project does not accept  plantation on disputed land and further highlighted that the  plantation must occur only on land with a clear customary land  right.  The plantation activity must be confined to the Project Area in  the Cropland and/or Grassland but must not occur on the  baseline Forestland. However, the Intersection Tool in the GIS  located overlap between Cumulative EPA 2017 (Cumulative EPA  2017.shp) and Forest land (Land_Use_Forest.shp) maps at 102  locations (Refer to Image 1). We also noticed that the

What is the number of native trees plant.content: : Conformance | that died out of the planted trees)  For annual tree count, the Project assigned the WOS  Coordinators in their respective area. The farmer and the WOS  Coordinator together counted the trees with GPS locations in the  farmland. The number of trees counted in a farmland is used for  the annual payment.   The GPS locations taken by the Auditor confirmed the plantation  areas within the Project Area. The GPS location of individual  trees provided evidence for the presence of trees on the  farmlands which were counted and accounted for calculating  carbon abatement.    When the participating farmers were asked the total number of  seedlings/trees counted in the last year’s annual count and the  payment they received, they were able to tell the number of  trees/seedlings survived and the payment received. These  numbers matched to the Project’s record in 2018.  In response to CAR 03/19 and CAR 09/19, the Project Owner  revised the shapefiles for cropland and grassland to account for  the trees only on the Eligible Planting Areas. Hence, the number  of trees in 2016 and 2017 were updated to 18,490 and 23,447  in the current version (v3) of the CO2 Certificate Calculation file  (GS4210 WOS Baguia CO2 Certificate Calculations_March 2019  v3.0.xlsx). As a result, the total tree count in 2016 was  significantly reduced to 18,490 from 27,580 in the previous  version (v2) of the CO2 Certificate Calculation file (GS4210 WOS  Baguia CO2 Certificate Calculations_March 2019 v2.0.xlsx) while  the tree count slightly reduced to 23,447 from 23,511 in 2017.   The tree count presented in the CO2 Certificate Calculation file  (GS4210 WOS Baguia CO2 Certificate Calculations_March 2019  v3.0.xlsx) did not match with the tree count in the maps in  years 2010 -2015. The cumulative number of trees reduced from  60,559 to 43,869 in the years 2010 to 2015 in the CO2  Certificate Calculation file of the previous verification (GS4210  Baseline CS and CO2 Fixation models PFA Review_Update  2017.xlsx) to this verification (GS4210 WOS Baguia CO2  Certificate Calculations_March 2019 v3.0.xlsx). The Auditors  accepted the Project Owner’s explanation regarding the  discrepancy in the annual tree count. However, the tree count in  the maps must reconcile with the number of trees accounted for  carbon certificate calculation. Hence, CAR 03/19 and CAR 09/19  are closed and FAR 03/19 is created that the Project Owner  applies the tree count number in the maps with GPS coordinates  to the CO2 Certificate Calculation file.: Yes                      No

What is the geographic scope of reforest.content:
Principle 1: Contribution to Climate Security & Sustainable Development 
The project is an eligible project type in the Land Use and Forestry (LUF) sector of the GSF Program.
The project activities are Afforestation/Reforestation (AR) of native and exotic non-invasive tree 
species on degraded cropland and grasslands by smallholder farmers in Timor Leste.
The Project’s 
AR activities are implemented according to the GS4GG Land Use and Forestry (LUF) Activity

date.
The project activities involve reforestation by planting a variety of native, and exotic non-
invasive species on smallholder subsistence farmer’s privately owned lands in the communities of 
Baguia, Timor Leste.
All eligible planting areas do not occur on wetland areas, or areas with organic 
soils.
Such areas aren’t very conducive to the mountainous landscape of Baguia.
Soil disturbance

It is the expectation that the project’s reforestation activities will reduce sub-soil salinity, soil 
erosion and overall improve water quality throughout the project area where the planting activities 
occur.
The auditor finds no reason to believe otherwise.

Risk Area 2: Audit Findings | A risk that the Project has not accurately mapped and estimated  the Planting Area.: The Planting Area (PA) represents the land area where the  participating farmers have customary and undisputed land rights  and have undertaken tree plantation activities since 2010. The  PA is derived by applying a buffer area of 5 m diameter around  each planted tree, representing an average width of tree crown  at maturity. The cumulative Planting Area was obtained by  merging the tree buffer area around each tree.   The interview with the stakeholders did not raise any issues on  the exclusion of areas of special interest such as spiritual,  cultural and social significance. However, the farmers raised an  issue on planting on the disputed land due to lack of a clear  recognition of customary right to a farmer. The WOS staff  clarified to the farmers that the Project does not accept  plantation on disputed land and further highlighted that the  plantation must occur only on land with a clear customary land  right.  The plantation activity must be confined to the Project Area in  the Cropland and/or Grassland but must not occur on the  baseline Forestland. However, the Intersection Tool in the GIS  located overlap between Cumulative EPA 2017 (Cumulative EPA  2017.shp) and Forest land (Land_Use_Forest.shp) maps at 102  locations (Refer to Image 1). Risk Area 5: Audit  Findings | The Project did not accurately measure the trees and did not  correctly identify tree species for accounting carbon credits.: Over a four day period, with the assistance of WOS staff and  others, the Auditor measured tree circumference and GPS  locations of 855 trees at 15 farmlands, including all planting  years between 2010 and 2017 except for the year 2013, on both  grassland and cropland and at low, medium and high elevations.  The Auditor observed a planting spacing of 3 to 5 m between the  planted trees.   Mahogany (Swietenia macrophylla) is the dominant species for  the Project Area. Besides, the Auditor also observed some  plantation areas comprising Casurina (C. Equisetofolia), Indian  Sandalwood (Santalum album), Teak (Tectona grandis) and Wild  Almond (Sterculia foetida). For the measurement of the sample  trees in 2018-19, only trees of Mahogany were measured and the  staff and farmer had correctly identified the species.   The significance test between the Auditor’s tree measurement  data and the PD’s tree measurement data did not provide any  evidence to suggest that the sample means from these two  datasets were significantly different, with the exception of the  year 2015. For trees planted in 2015, the Auditor’s data  calculated a higher mean diameter than the PD’s data. Since the  PD had used a low value for mean diameter for the trees in 2015,  it is reasonable to say that the value is conservative and does not  overestimate the carbon stock.  Based on the data and test of significance, the Auditors conclude  that the PD’s tree measurement data were accurately measured.

However, we observed a discrepancy in the tree count for the planting years 2010 to  2015 between the revised maps and the CO2 Certificate Calculation file. As a result,  the CO2 Certificate Calculation file (GS4210 WOS Baguia CO2 Certificate  Calculations_March 2019 v3.0.xlsx) had a cumulative tree count of 85,806 as of  2017 with 4,492 trees more than the total trees in the revised maps for Cropland  and Grassland (Cropland Tree Count_2017.shp and Grassland Tree  Count_2017.shp) with a combined tree count of 81,314 in the cumulative tree count  as of 2017. In a response to the Auditor’s clarification request on this issue, the  Project Owner explained that the annual tree count is a discrete event and might  differ each year because farmers might not count trees or trees are not included  because of a dispute between farmers. The Project Owner accepted this issue and  claimed to address this by using RFID technology for accounting each tree with a  unique ID in the next tree count.

Risk Area 5: Audit  Findings | The Project did not accurately measure the trees and did not  correctly identify tree species for accounting carbon credits.: Over a four day period, with the assistance of WOS staff and  others, the Auditor measured tree circumference and GPS  locations of 855 trees at 15 farmlands, including all planting  years between 2010 and 2017 except for the year 2013, on both  grassland and cropland and at low, medium and high elevations.  The Auditor observed a planting spacing of 3 to 5 m between the  planted trees.   Mahogany (Swietenia macrophylla) is the dominant species for  the Project Area. Besides, the Auditor also observed some  plantation areas comprising Casurina (C. Equisetofolia), Indian  Sandalwood (Santalum album), Teak (Tectona grandis) and Wild  Almond (Sterculia foetida). For the measurement of the sample  trees in 2018-19, only trees of Mahogany were measured and the  staff and farmer had correctly identified the species.   The significance test between the Auditor’s tree measurement  data and the PD’s tree measurement data did not provide any  evidence to suggest that the sample means from these two  datasets were significantly different, with the exception of the  year 2015. For trees planted in 2015, the Auditor’s data  calculated a higher mean diameter than the PD’s data. Since the  PD had used a low value for mean diameter for the trees in 2015,  it is reasonable to say that the value is conservative and does not  overestimate the carbon stock.  Based on the data and test of significance, the Auditors conclude  that the PD’s tree measurement data were accurately measured.

: Conformance | for estimating the carbon stock.   The Project has tree plantations with age cohorts from 2010 to  2018 at various elevations, aspects and soil conditions. A test of  significance in the diameter data collected during the Auditor’s  field visit inferred that the tree growth of Mahogany trees with  the same planting year (e.g. 2010) significantly different at  three different elevations at 5% significance level. The farmers  and WOS staff have also acknowledged the slower growth of  Mahogany trees in the high elevation than in the low elevation.  For this reason, the farmers are demanding a fast growing tree  species on the high elevation.  A CAR 02/19 was raised on this issue seeking an explanation  and justification on how the Project meets the requirement for  identifying the MUs with homogenous characteristics as per the  requirement in 200-GS4GG-Land-Use-Forests-Activity- Requirements-v1.1 on page 3 and pages 28-29.  In response to the CAR 02/19, the Project explained and  justified the current stratification strategy and approach and also  advised that the Project will adapt the RFID technology and  install the system in all trees in between June – October 2019  for accounting CO2 Fixation in each tree in the Project Area. The  Auditors agree that the Project does not need stratification if all  trees are measured and accounted for carbon sequestration.  However, we have a concern that the technology may not be  implemented to the entire Project Area within the next  verification.   FAR 01/19 is created to ensure that the Project has identified  Modelling Units to comply with the requirement as set out in the  GS4GG A/R methodology. If the Project has not rolled out RFID  technology to all trees in the Project Area, the Project must  analyse for the significance difference in the tree growth rates at  different elevations and site conditions (cropland vs grassland)  and appropriately stratify the Project Area into Modelling Units.: Yes                      No
